stops.name: Hereford Cathedral
stops.shortDescription: Home to the Mappa Mundi and the world’s largest chained library — a city of faith built on the bones of a martyred king
stops.narration:
Every great English city has a building that tells you what it is. In Hereford, that building is this one.

The Cathedral was founded in 676 AD on the site where St Ethelbert, King of East Anglia, was buried after his murder. He had come to Mercia to negotiate a marriage and was killed by his host, Offa the Great. Ethelbert became a martyr, his tomb became a pilgrimage site, and the Cathedral that grew around it became one of the most important churches in western England.

The building you see now is largely Norman and later medieval, built in the distinctive local red sandstone. Unlike the pale limestone of Canterbury or the oolite of Bath, this stone was quarried from the hills of the Welsh Marches, and it makes the Cathedral look and feel like part of the landscape it stands in.

Inside, two things above all others are worth seeking out. The Mappa Mundi, made around 1300, is the largest surviving medieval map of the world — not a map in any modern sense, but a diagram of Christian cosmology with Jerusalem at the centre, Paradise at the top, and monsters at the margins.

The Chained Library contains over 1,500 books, each secured by a chain to its shelf. It is the largest intact chained library in the world. This was the medieval internet: everything worth knowing, locked in one room, available to those with the knowledge to read it.

In 1988 the Cathedral nearly lost the Mappa Mundi. Facing serious financial difficulties, the Dean and Chapter explored selling it to an American buyer. The resulting public outcry was immediate and enormous — a fundraising campaign raised millions in weeks. The map stayed. It is now housed in its own purpose-built gallery, opened in 1996.

Every August the Cathedral becomes the home of the Three Choirs Festival — rotating since the 1720s between Hereford, Worcester, and Gloucester. Elgar premiered work here. Vaughan Williams came every year he could. The festival continues to this day.

Stand in the nave and look east. The light comes through the great window at the end of the choir in colours that the medieval masons planned for precisely this effect. They had never seen the building complete. They built it anyway.

Take a slow walk around the Cathedral now — step inside if you can. Stand in the Chained Library, find the Mappa Mundi, watch the light fall through the choir window. Take it all in, take your time. When you've had your fill, I'll meet you outside and we'll head together to the Bishop's Palace.
facts: The Mappa Mundi was made around 1300 and in 1988 was nearly sold to an American buyer. A public fundraising campaign raised millions in weeks and kept it in Hereford permanently.

The Chained Library contains over 1,500 books each secured to its shelf by a chain — long enough to allow the book to be read on the desk in front. It is the largest intact chained library in the world.

The Three Choirs Festival, rotating between Hereford, Worcester and Gloucester since the 1720s, is one of the oldest classical music festivals in the world. Elgar and Vaughan Williams were regular attendees.

stops.name: The Bishop’s Palace
stops.shortDescription: A medieval palace hidden behind the Cathedral — and gardens that have been here since the Normans
stops.narration:
Just to the south of the Cathedral, down a lane that most visitors miss, is one of the most complete medieval episcopal complexes in England. The Bishop's Palace has been here since the Norman period — over nine hundred years of continuous occupation on the same site.

The great hall, built around 1179 by Bishop Robert Foliot, is still standing — its proportions those of a serious Norman building, designed to impress visiting nobility and demonstrate the power of the diocese. Hereford was not a peripheral see: it controlled a diocese that ran from the Welsh borders deep into the Marches.

The palace gardens slope gently down towards the River Wye. On a clear day the views across the water to the meadows on the far bank are the kind that make you understand immediately why people have been living in this valley for thousands of years.

The gardens contain a yew tree said to be over five hundred years old — which would make it already ancient when Henry VIII was dissolving the monasteries. Trees this size make the medieval period feel present rather than distant.

The Wye Valley has long drawn poets and artists. William Wordsworth made his journey through this valley in 1798 and wrote Lines Written a Few Miles above Tintern Abbey directly inspired by the landscape. Turner painted the Wye. Gilpin coined the word picturesque for it. The valley's pull on the imagination is not a modern invention.

The gardens have been carefully restored and are now more openly accessible to visitors than at any point in the twentieth century. On a fine afternoon, they are one of Hereford's genuinely lovely surprises.

Wander down through the gardens if they're open, look across to the meadows on the far bank, and put your hand on that ancient yew if you can find it. Whenever you're ready, we'll move on to the line of the old city walls.
facts: The great hall of the Bishop’s Palace dates from around 1179 — one of the oldest surviving episcopal halls in England.
William Wordsworth visited the Wye Valley in 1798 and wrote Lines Written a Few Miles above Tintern Abbey directly inspired by the river and landscape visible from this area.
The palace gardens have been carefully restored and are now more openly accessible to visitors than at any point in the twentieth century.

stops.name: Hereford City Walls & Redcliffe Gardens
stops.shortDescription: The medieval walls that once ringed the city — and the gardens that grew from their rubble
stops.narration:
Hereford was a walled city from at least the tenth century. The Anglo-Saxon burh was enclosed by earthworks and a defensive ditch. The Normans rebuilt these defences in stone after the Conquest. The resulting walls ran for approximately a mile around the city, pierced by gateways and defended by towers.

Most of the walls were demolished in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries as the city expanded and the stone was needed for other projects. What remains today is fragmentary — sections preserved where the demolition was incomplete or the stone proved too well-built to shift easily.

The gardens along this stretch preserve the line of the old city defences. The ground level changes here reflect the original earthwork — you can still read the profile of the medieval rampart in the slope of the garden.

Hereford's walls were tested. The city was a Royalist stronghold during the Civil War, besieged by Scottish forces under the Earl of Leven in 1645 for over a month. The walls held during that siege — but the city fell to a different Parliamentary force later the same year.

During the Second World War, Hereford received thousands of evacuee children from Birmingham and the industrial cities. The city's population temporarily swelled, old buildings near these walls became temporary homes for children who had never seen proper countryside. Some never went back.

The gardens were significantly improved in the early 2000s and are now a proper public green space — maintained, planted, and used by local residents in a way the neglected wall line never was. Sometimes the best thing you can do with a medieval ruin is make a garden around it.

Walk the length of these gardens — the old wall line is there if you know how to read it. Sit a moment, picture the rampart, picture the besieging army on the far side. When you're ready, we'll head down towards Castle Green and the river.
facts: Hereford was besieged by Scottish forces in 1645 during the Civil War. The walls held during that siege — but the city fell to a different Parliamentary force later the same year.
During the Second World War, Hereford received thousands of child evacuees from Birmingham and the industrial cities. Some families stayed and became part of the city permanently.
The city’s five medieval gateways — Friars Gate, Eign Gate, St Owen’s Gate, Wye Bridge Gate and Widemarsh Gate — were all demolished in the eighteenth century.

stops.name: Castle Green
stops.shortDescription: Where Hereford’s great castle stood for five hundred years — now a park with the best view of the river
stops.narration:
The large open space you are standing on was, for five hundred years, one of the most strategically important castle sites in England.

Hereford Castle was founded by William FitzOsbern, one of William the Conqueror's closest companions, shortly after the Norman Conquest. Its position was chosen with care: on a loop of the River Wye, with water on three sides, commanding the crossing point and the road into Wales. Whoever held Hereford Castle held the key to England's western frontier.

The castle was large, heavily fortified, and repeatedly fought over. King Stephen was captured nearby during the Anarchy. King John stored part of his treasury here. Edward I used it as a staging post for his Welsh campaigns. The castle was at the centre of English power in the March for three centuries.

In the seventeenth century, having survived medieval siege warfare and the Civil War, the castle was simply demolished — not in battle, but in peacetime, by a government that decided it was too expensive to maintain. The stone was sold off and within a generation the most important castle in the West of England had disappeared.

The Nelson Column in the middle of the Green predates the famous one in Trafalgar Square by thirty-two years. Erected in 1809, four years after Trafalgar — Hereford mourned its admiral fast. London's column didn't follow until 1843.

Today Castle Green is the city's main civic park — used for the annual Hereford Food Festival, summer concerts, and on any dry afternoon, the full range of city life: runners, families, dogs, and the occasional heron standing completely still in the shallows below.

The view across the Wye from the Green is essentially unchanged from what the castle garrison would have seen. The same river, the same meadows, the same hills on the horizon. That continuity is one of the things that makes the Welsh Marches unusual.

Take a few minutes here. Walk across to the Nelson Column, sit on the grass if it's a fine day, watch the boats pass below. When you're ready, we'll head back into the heart of the city to find the Museum and Art Gallery.
facts: Hereford’s Nelson Column was erected in 1809 — thirty-two years before the more famous column in Trafalgar Square. Hereford mourned its admiral fast.
Hereford Castle was one of the most besieged in medieval England, controlling the main road into Wales. Almost nothing survives above ground — the mound is the only visible trace.
Castle Green now hosts the annual Hereford Food Festival each autumn — one of the best regional food events in the West of England.

stops.name: Hereford Museum & Art Gallery
stops.shortDescription: Roman mosaics, a world-class cider story, and the natural history of an exceptional county — all free
stops.narration:
The museum on Broad Street is the place where Hereford's long history is most systematically gathered and presented. The building itself is Victorian Gothic — built in 1874, with the ornate stonework and pitched roofs that the period favoured for civic institutions.

The Roman material comes largely from Magnis — the Roman town at Kenchester, about five miles west of Hereford. Magnis was a civitas capital: the administrative centre of the Dobunni tribe under Roman rule, a town with a forum, a basilica, and the kind of urban infrastructure the Romans built wherever they governed.

One thing the museum documents particularly well is the cider industry — Herefordshire's most characteristic contribution to British culture. The H.P. Bulmer company was founded in Hereford in 1887 and grew to become the largest cider producer in the world. The ancient orchards you can see from any high point in the county — small, gnarled, moss-covered trees in a density that has disappeared almost everywhere else — are the visible evidence of that history.

The natural history collections document the county's remarkable biodiversity. Herefordshire sits in one of the most ecologically rich corners of England, where the geology changes, the rivers are clean, and the hedgerows have survived in a density that has largely disappeared elsewhere.

The art gallery holds changing exhibitions and a permanent collection strong in local landscapes. Herefordshire has attracted painters since the eighteenth century — the valley light, the orchards, the particular quality of an agricultural county that is always beautiful has made it a subject that rewards sustained attention.

Free entry. Worth an hour of your time. On a rainy afternoon in Hereford, the museum is one of the better ways to spend two hours.

If the weather's against you, this is the place to spend a proper hour. If not, take a quick look at the Roman material and step back outside. Whenever you're ready, we'll cross the road to All Saints Church.
facts: Kenchester (Roman Magnis), five miles west of Hereford, was a proper Roman town with forum and basilica — almost entirely unexcavated. The objects here represent a tiny fraction of what is still underground.
H.P. Bulmer, founded in Hereford in 1887, became the world’s largest cider producer. The Bulmers Norman apple used in Strongbow was developed on local Herefordshire farms.
Herefordshire has more ancient orchards per square mile than almost any other county in England — the visual evidence of a cider-making tradition stretching back centuries.

stops.name: All Saints Church
stops.shortDescription: A medieval church with a twisted spire, a chained library, and one of England’s most celebrated church cafés
stops.narration:
All Saints Church stands at the corner of Broad Street and High Street — a thirteenth-century church in the middle of a modern city, and one of the most quietly surprising buildings in Hereford.

The church was begun in the late thirteenth century and completed in stages through the fourteenth. The nave is tall and light, the columns slender, the windows generous with daylight. It has the feel of a building that was always meant to be used — a working parish church for the merchants and craftspeople of the medieval city.

The most striking feature is the twisted spire. Church spires sometimes twist as the timber frame dries and shrinks unevenly over decades — pulling the whole structure slightly out of true. All Saints' spire has had a pronounced and permanent twist for centuries. Simon Jenkins listed this church in England's Thousand Best Churches partly on the strength of it.

The church also has a small chained library — thirty-three volumes, mostly seventeenth-century, chained to their shelves in the same way as the far larger collection at the Cathedral. Two chained libraries within a few hundred metres of each other is exceptional in England.

Since 1999, the nave has housed one of England's most celebrated church cafés — widely credited as a pioneer of the concept that has since been copied by hundreds of churches across the country. Good coffee, good food, in a medieval building. The pews are gone, but the columns, the windows, and the height of the ceiling are exactly as they were.

In 2020, during the COVID lockdowns, All Saints live-streamed concerts from this medieval nave to audiences around the world. A building begun in the thirteenth century found a global audience via broadband. That combination of the very old and the very new is something this church manages unusually well.

Step inside if it's open — the café is worth a coffee, and the columns are worth ten minutes of just looking up. When you're ready, we'll move into the heart of the city, to the market square at High Town.
facts: All Saints’ twisted spire is listed in Simon Jenkins’ England’s Thousand Best Churches. The twist developed as the timber frame dried unevenly over centuries — and is now permanent.
The café at All Saints opened in 1999 and is widely credited as one of the first church cafés in England — a model that hundreds of churches across the country have since copied.
During the COVID lockdowns of 2020, All Saints live-streamed concerts from the medieval nave that were watched by thousands of people around the world.

stops.name: High Town & the Market
stops.shortDescription: The commercial heart of Hereford since before the Normans — and still the place where the city does its business
stops.narration:
You are standing in the commercial heart of Hereford. High Town has been the city's market place since at least the eleventh century — the Saxons who established the burh needed a market, and this broad open space at the centre of the settlement was the obvious place for it.

The buildings around the edges represent several centuries of prosperous urban architecture. The black and white timbered buildings are typical of the Herefordshire vernacular: oak-framed structures built from the local timber that was abundant in a heavily wooded county. Herefordshire has the highest density of black and white timber-framed buildings of any county in England.

The indoor Buttermarket has been on this site since the medieval period, though the current building dates from the 1860s. The function is the same as it has always been: a place to buy things, to meet people, to hear news, to do business.

Hereford remains a genuine market town. The farmers' market — held on the first and third Saturdays of the month — attracts producers from across the county. Cheese, cider, meat, vegetables, local honey. It is one of the better farmers' markets in the West of England.

The Christmas Market has grown significantly in recent years and now fills the whole of High Town with traders from across the region. Hereford regularly appears in quality-of-life surveys as one of the most liveable small cities in England — combining city amenities with immediate access to exceptional countryside.

Spend a few minutes looking at the buildings on the edges of High Town. The variety of styles — medieval timber-framing, Georgian brick, Victorian stucco — is the visual record of a city that has been continuously commercial for a thousand years.

Spend a few minutes wandering. Look at the buildings, look at the people, get a feel for the rhythm of the place. When you've had your moment, we'll find Hereford's most photographed building — and it's right in front of you.
facts: Hereford’s Tuesday market has been documented since at least the twelfth century — one of the oldest continuously operating markets in England.

Herefordshire has the highest density of black and white timber-framed buildings of any county in England. The abundance of local oak made it the dominant building material until the eighteenth century.
Hereford regularly features in quality-of-life surveys as one of the most liveable small cities in England — combining genuine city amenities with immediate access to exceptional countryside.

stops.name: Old House Museum
stops.shortDescription: The most photographed building in Hereford — a Jacobean townhouse that survived while everything around it was demolished
stops.narration:
In the middle of High Town, surrounded by shopfronts and pedestrians, stands a building from a completely different world. The Old House was built in 1621 as part of a long row of shops and houses lining the medieval market street. Everything either side of it was demolished in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. The Old House alone survived.

The date 1621 puts it squarely in the Jacobean period — James I had been on the throne eighteen years, Shakespeare had been dead for five, the Civil War was still twenty years away. The construction technique is known as close-studding: vertical oak timbers placed close together, the gaps filled with plaster panels. The upper stories jet out over the ground floor — the jettying that maximised floor space on a valuable street plot.

Inside, the museum recreates Jacobean domestic life across three floors. The rooms are furnished with period pieces — four-poster beds, pewter plates, heavy wooden furniture designed to last generations. The scale is intimate: ceilings low, rooms small, windows leaded.

In March 2020, during the first COVID lockdown, photographs of the Old House standing completely alone in an empty High Town spread across social media and were shared thousands of times. The image of this seventeenth-century building isolated in a deserted pedestrian street had an unexpected resonance — it looked like something from a dream. The Old House became briefly a global image of lockdown.

It remains Hereford's most photographed building. The shot from the northeast corner — the jettied upper stories against the sky, the black and white timbers sharp in morning light — is the image most associated with this city. The building survived because it was always too useful to demolish. That accidental survival is now its most reliable ambassador.

Walk all the way around it — the building looks different from every angle, and you'll see things from one side you simply can't see from the others. Step inside if the museum is open. When you're ready, we'll head north to a quiet corner most visitors never find: the ruins of Blackfriars.
facts: The Old House was built in 1621 as part of Butchers’ Row. The rest of the row was demolished in 1816 — the Old House survived because it was always too useful to knock down.
During the first COVID lockdown in 2020, photographs of the Old House standing completely alone in an empty High Town went viral, shared thousands of times around the world.
The house was gifted to Hereford city by Lloyds Bank in 1928. Before that it had served as a bank, a butcher’s shop, and a private residence.

stops.name: Blackfriars Ruin
stops.shortDescription: A Dominican friary, a rare medieval preaching cross, and the best-kept secret in Hereford
stops.narration:
A short walk north from the city centre, in a small public garden on Widemarsh Street, stand the ruins of a Dominican friary. The Black Friars came to Hereford in 1276 and remained active in the city's religious life for nearly three hundred years.

The Dominicans were the great preaching order of the medieval church — scholars and teachers who moved through cities, educated the clergy, and engaged with the intellectual questions of the age. Their house in Hereford was one of the more important in the Welsh March.

Henry VIII dissolved it in 1538. The buildings were stripped, materials carted off, and what remained fell into ruin. What you see now is the shell of the nave and the outline of the cloister walk.

The outstanding survival is the preaching cross — a late medieval stone cross standing in what was the friary cemetery. Only a handful of preaching crosses survive in England. This one is in unusually good condition: the steps, base, and shaft are intact. Stand beside it and think about outdoor preaching in a fourteenth-century cemetery, the congregation gathered among the graves with the church behind them.

The garden around the ruins is now partly managed by Herefordshire Wildlife Trust. The combination of old stonework and deliberately relaxed planting makes it a habitat as well as a historic site. Slow worms have been found in the warm south-facing walls.

Blackfriars is the least-visited significant historic site in Hereford city centre — almost entirely unknown to visitors and coach parties. No ticket, no gift shop. On a weekday afternoon you may have the garden entirely to yourself. That is either a recommendation or a description, depending on what you are looking for.

Take your time here. Sit in the garden if it's quiet, walk around the preaching cross, look for the cloister outline in the grass. When you're ready, we'll head down to the river — the last stop on our walk together.
facts: The Hereford Blackfriars preaching cross is one of only a handful of intact medieval preaching crosses remaining in England — most were destroyed during the Reformation.
The garden around the ruins is partly managed by Herefordshire Wildlife Trust. Slow worms have been recorded in the warm south-facing stonework.
Blackfriars is the least-visited significant historic site in Hereford city centre — almost entirely unknown to visitors and coach parties.

stops.name: The River Wye & Wye Bridge
stops.shortDescription: Where the walk ends and the river begins — two thousand years of Hereford at the water’s edge
stops.narration:
We end here, at the water, because the River Wye is where Hereford begins.

The city exists because of this crossing. The Romans had a ford here. The Saxons built their burh to control it. The Normans built their castle to defend it. Every significant event in Hereford's history has been shaped by this river — the road from England to Wales had to cross here, and whoever controlled the crossing controlled the trade, the taxes, and the movement of armies.

The bridge you are looking at is stone, dating from the fourteenth and fifteenth centuries — though repaired and widened many times since. It replaced an earlier wooden structure, which replaced the Saxon ford. The river has been crossed at roughly this point for two thousand years.

The River Wye is one of the cleanest large rivers in England, designated a Site of Special Scientific Interest for its entire length. Otters have returned in significant numbers after being locally extinct for most of the twentieth century. You are unlikely to see one from the bridge in daylight — but at dusk, standing quietly on the bank and watching the water, the chances improve considerably.

The Wye is also one of the finest canoe rivers in Britain. The stretch from Hay-on-Wye to Hereford — about twenty miles — is paddled by thousands of people each year, supported by a network of hire and camping businesses along the route. The river that defended this city for a thousand years now draws people to it for entirely different reasons.

Hereford has shown you today what several centuries of English history looks like in a medium-sized city that most people drive past on the way somewhere else. I hope it has convinced you to stop for longer next time. Come back. The Cathedral is always worth returning to. The river is always worth sitting beside.
facts: The River Wye is designated a Site of Special Scientific Interest for its entire length — one of the cleanest large rivers in England.
Otters have returned to the Wye in significant numbers after being locally extinct for most of the twentieth century. Dawn and dusk on the riverbank give the best chance of a sighting.
The twenty-mile canoe route from Hay-on-Wye to Hereford is one of the finest river journeys in Britain, paddled by thousands of visitors each year.

locationFacts.text: Hereford Cathedral's Mappa Mundi was nearly sold to an American buyer in 1988. The sale fell through due to public outcry, and a fundraising campaign eventually secured it for the Cathedral. Had it sold, it would have left England permanently.

The Three Choirs Festival - one of the oldest classical music festivals in the world, dating to the 1720s - rotates annually between Hereford, Gloucester, and Worcester. When it comes to Hereford, the Cathedral is its home.

Hereford is the birthplace of the Hereford cattle breed - the red and white beef cattle that now number over five million worldwide. The breed was developed on local farms in the eighteenth century and transformed beef farming across the English-speaking world.

The SAS - the Special Air Service - is based at Credenhill, just outside Hereford. The regiment has been associated with the city since it was formally established after the Second World War. Hereford is the only city in Britain with an active SAS barracks.

Nell Gwynne, mistress of Charles II and mother of the Duke of St Albans, is traditionally said to have been born in Hereford around 1650. Her exact birthplace is disputed, but the city has claimed her for centuries.

Roger of Hereford was one of the most important mathematicians and astronomers of twelfth-century England. Working from this city, he produced astronomical tables and writings on astrology that were used across medieval Europe for two centuries.

The term 'marches' - as in the Welsh Marches, the borderlands between England and Wales - comes from the Latin marca, meaning boundary or frontier. For over four centuries after the Norman Conquest, Herefordshire was governed by the Lords Marcher, who had special powers unavailable to ordinary English nobles.

The apple variety Bulmers Norman, used in the production of Strongbow cider, was developed near Hereford in the nineteenth century. The H.P. Bulmer company was founded in Hereford in 1887 and remains one of the largest cider producers in the world.

Hereford was the last English cathedral city to get a railway connection - the line from Shrewsbury arrived in 1853, making it briefly the most isolated major city in England. The delay was partly due to the difficulty of routing a railway through the surrounding hills.

The meadows visible across the River Wye from the city are traditional hay meadows - among the most biodiverse habitats in lowland Britain. They have been cut for hay every summer for centuries, maintaining a plant community that has largely disappeared from intensively farmed landscapes.

The diocese of Hereford, established in the seventh century, was one of the first in England west of the Severn. It was created to serve the territory of the Magonsaete - the Anglo-Saxon sub-kingdom occupying what is now Herefordshire and south Shropshire.

Herefordshire has more miles of hedgerow per square mile than any other county in England. Many of these hedges are ancient - some contain species combinations that indicate they have been in place since the medieval period or earlier.

St Thomas Cantilupe, Bishop of Hereford from 1275 to 1282, was the last English-born medieval saint to be canonised before the Reformation. His shrine in the Cathedral was one of the most visited pilgrimage sites in England in the fourteenth century.

The Herefordshire School of Romanesque sculpture - a distinctive tradition of carved stone decoration found in churches across the county - produced some of the most imaginative architectural sculpture in twelfth-century England. The best examples can be seen at Kilpeck, twelve miles south of the city.

The county of Herefordshire was briefly abolished in 1974 when it was merged with Worcestershire to form Hereford and Worcester. So unpopular was this arrangement that the county was restored in 1998 - one of the very few administrative reversals of the 1974 local government reorganisation.

sponsors.name: The Nest Hereford
sponsors.tagline: Specialty coffee and local food in the Cathedral Quarter

sponsors.name: The Bookshop by the River
sponsors.tagline: New, used and local books with views of the Wye

events.name: Hereford May Fair
events.upcoming: The Hereford May Fair is coming up soon — one of England’s oldest chartered fairs, it’s been held in the city since the thirteenth century. Worth planning a return visit around.
events.recent: The Hereford May Fair has just taken place — one of the oldest chartered fairs in England, a tradition going back to the thirteenth century.
events.during: The Hereford May Fair is on right now in the city — one of England’s oldest chartered fairs. You may hear it from here.

events.name: Three Choirs Festival
events.upcoming: The Three Choirs Festival is coming to Hereford soon — one of the oldest classical music festivals in the world, rotating between Hereford, Worcester and Gloucester since the 1720s. The Cathedral will be its home.
events.recent: The Three Choirs Festival has just finished in Hereford — one of the oldest classical music festivals in the world.
events.during: The Three Choirs Festival is happening in Hereford right now — the Cathedral and city are at the heart of one of the world’s oldest classical music festivals.

events.name: Hereford Food Festival
events.upcoming: The Hereford Food Festival is coming up on Castle Green — one of the best regional food events in the West of England, celebrating the county’s exceptional producers.
events.recent: The Hereford Food Festival has just taken place on Castle Green — a brilliant celebration of the county’s food and drink producers.
events.during: The Hereford Food Festival is on right now on Castle Green — well worth a visit if you have time after the tour.

events.name: Big River Festival
events.upcoming: The Big River Festival is coming up — Hereford’s celebration of the River Wye with events, walks, and water activities.
events.recent: The Big River Festival has just finished — Hereford’s annual celebration of the River Wye.
events.during: The Big River Festival is happening in Hereford right now — celebrating the River Wye with events and activities across the city.
